title: Before the Pyramids
bullets: The Maya built their greatest cities in the Peten, a dense limestone jungle with almost no surface water, where sustaining large populations required aggressive reservoir engineering and raised-field agriculture.
Cuello in Belize, occupied from around 1200 BCE, represents the earliest known Maya community with evidence of maize farming and pottery production.
Nakbe in the Peten developed monumental stone platforms and pyramids around 800 BCE, the earliest large-scale Maya architecture, connected by trade to highland obsidian and jade sources.
El Mirador's La Danta pyramid complex rises 236 feet above the jungle floor, and its volume exceeds the Great Pyramid of Giza, though it sits partly on a natural hill.
Sacbeob, raised limestone causeways surfaced with crushed white stone, connected Preclassic cities across the jungle, with the longest running 62 miles from Coba to Yaxuna.
The San Bartolo murals, dating to roughly 100 BCE, depict the Hero Twins myth and the Maize God in vivid polychrome, the oldest known intact Maya paintings.
The Maya adopted the Long Count calendar around the first century BCE, a linear system counting days from a mythological creation date of August 11, 3114 BCE.
El Mirador collapsed around 150 CE, probably from deforestation and drought, leaving a power vacuum in the Peten that Tikal would fill.

title: The Classic Dawn
bullets: The Classic period began when the Maya started carving detailed historical texts on stone stelae, recording named kings, battles, and rituals for the first time in the Americas.
Tikal grew into a metropolis of 60,000 to 100,000 people covering 47 square miles, its skyline dominated by limestone pyramids up to 212 feet tall, all built without metal tools or wheeled transport.
Around 350 CE the Maya independently invented zero, represented by a shell glyph, one of only three or four civilizations in human history to develop the concept.
Maya astronomers calculated the synodic period of Venus at 584 days, an error of less than two hours from the modern value, and used Venus appearances to time warfare.
Cacao beans became a standardized currency across the Maya lowlands, solving the problem of exchange in a society without metal coinage.
On January 16, 378 CE, a figure named Siyaj K'ahk' arrived at Tikal representing Teotihuacan, and the existing king died the same day, replaced by a new ruler in Teotihuacan-style regalia.
K'inich Yax K'uk' Mo' founded the dynasty of Copan in 426 CE, and K'uk' Bahlam I founded Palenque's dynasty in 431 CE, establishing the city-state constellation of the Classic Maya world.
Every Maya city was ruled by a k'uhul ajaw, a holy lord claiming divine authority, but no single ruler ever unified the Maya into an empire.

title: The Great Rivalry
bullets: The superpower rivalry between Tikal and Calakmul, sitting 60 miles apart in the Peten jungle, dominated Maya politics for over 150 years and drew in every major lowland city.
In 562 CE, Calakmul's ally Caracol launched a ritually sanctioned star war against Tikal, beginning a 130-year silence during which Tikal erected almost no dated monuments.
Yuknoom Ch'een II assembled the most extensive vassal network in Maya history from Calakmul, marrying daughters to allied kings and installing friendly rulers on defeated thrones.
A Tikal prince named B'alaj Chan K'awiil defected to found Dos Pilas, then claimed Tikal's emblem glyph while fighting for Tikal's mortal enemy Calakmul.
In 695 CE, Jasaw Chan K'awiil I of Tikal defeated Calakmul's ruler Yich'aak K'ahk', breaking Calakmul's dominance and launching a massive rebuilding program including Temple I.
The Maya never built an empire because jungle logistics limited campaign range, every city needed its own divine king, and no conquest was ever administratively sustainable.
The Maya city-state system parallels ancient Greece more than Rome: dozens of competing poleis producing extraordinary culture through rivalry, with no victory ever permanent.

title: The Golden Age
bullets: K'inich Janaab Pakal ruled Palenque for 68 years, and his funerary pyramid, the Temple of the Inscriptions, contains the longest continuous Maya hieroglyphic text known at 617 glyph blocks.
Pakal's jade-masked sarcophagus lid depicts him falling into the jaws of the earth while the World Tree rises above, one of the most famous artworks in pre-Columbian America.

Lintel 24 at Yaxchilan shows Lady K'abal Xook pulling a thorn-studded rope through her tongue in a bloodletting ritual, one of the most vivid depictions of Maya royal women in the archaeological record.
Copan's king 18 Rabbit commissioned near-freestanding portrait stelae unique in the Maya world, then was captured and beheaded in 738 CE by his own vassal state Quirigua.
After killing 18 Rabbit, Quirigua erected the tallest freestanding stelae in the Maya world, including Stela E at over 33 feet tall and 65 tons.
Maya artists invented Maya Blue, a synthetic pigment made from indigo and palygorskite clay that resists fading for over a thousand years, one of the earliest synthetic pigments.
Lady Six Sky arrived at Naranjo around 682 CE from Dos Pilas and rebuilt it into a regional power, directing military campaigns and commissioning monuments in her own name.
The Bonampak murals, painted around 790 CE, cover three rooms with vivid scenes of battle, prisoner capture, and victory celebration, the finest surviving example of Classic Maya painting.

title: The Collapse
bullets: Between roughly 800 and 1000 CE, the Maya cities of the southern lowlands stopped building, stopped writing, and were progressively abandoned, their pyramids swallowed by jungle.
Paleoclimatic evidence from lake sediment cores and cave stalagmites shows severe multi-decadal droughts around 810, 860, and 910 CE, with rainfall possibly dropping 40 to 50 percent below normal.
The droughts hit a landscape already heavily deforested, densely populated with perhaps 3 to 5 million people in the Peten alone, and dependent on intensive agriculture with no margin for error.
The last known Long Count date in the southern lowlands was carved at Tonina in 909 CE, ending the system that had made Maya civilization the most historically self-conscious in the ancient Americas.

Puuc-style cities like Uxmal flourished in the northern Yucatan during the Terminal Classic, featuring the Pyramid of the Magician and the Governor's Palace with its 20,000 individually carved facade stones.
The Classic Maya Collapse was not a disappearance but a political collapse: the Maya people persisted, adapted, and built new societies in the northern Yucatan while the southern cities decayed.
The collapse parallels Rome's fall in timescale and complexity but differs fundamentally: dozens of independent city-states collapsed individually rather than one centralized empire fragmenting.

title: The Postclassic World
bullets: Chichen Itza dominated the early Postclassic Maya world from roughly 900 to 1100 CE, blending Maya and central Mexican architectural traditions in ways scholars still debate.
El Castillo at Chichen Itza has 91 steps on each of its four sides plus the top platform, totaling 365, and on equinoxes the afternoon sun casts serpent-shaped shadows down the northern staircase.
The Sacred Cenote at Chichen Itza yielded jade from Guatemala, gold from Panama and Colombia, and human remains, mapping Postclassic Mesoamerican trade in its offerings.
Mayapan ruled the Yucatan from roughly 1200 to 1441 CE through a confederacy of noble houses enforced by the Cocom dynasty, who kept allied lords as de facto hostages within its walls.
Maya coastal trading canoes, carved from single mahogany or ceiba trunks and paddled by crews of up to 25 men, carried cacao, obsidian, salt, and cotton along routes from Tabasco to Honduras.
The Xiu faction sacked Mayapan in 1441 CE, massacring the Cocom lords and ending the last centralized Maya political authority before the Spanish arrived.
The Dresden Codex, compiled around 1200 CE, is the oldest surviving book from the Americas, containing astronomical tables for predicting eclipses and tracking Venus with extraordinary accuracy.
Columbus encountered a Maya trading canoe off Honduras in 1502 CE, the first recorded European contact with Maya civilization, but he sailed on looking for China.

title: Words, Stars, and Zero
bullets: Maya hieroglyphic writing is the only fully developed script in the pre-Columbian Americas, a logosyllabary that could record any spoken sentence, comparable in power to Sumerian cuneiform or Egyptian hieroglyphs.
Soviet linguist Yuri Knorosov proposed in the 1950s that Maya glyphs had phonetic values, challenging the dominant theory and launching a decipherment that by the 1990s could read most Maya texts.

David Stuart began contributing to the decipherment as a teenager and won a MacArthur Fellowship at age 18, reading hundreds of previously opaque glyphs and proving the script was far more phonetically sophisticated than realized.
The Venus tables in the Dresden Codex track 65 Venus cycles across roughly 104 years, averaging the synodic period at 583.92 days, matching the modern value to within minutes per cycle.

The Maya vigesimal number system used just three symbols: a dot for 1, a bar for 5, and a shell glyph for zero, enabling positional notation that made their calendar and astronomical calculations possible.

The Long Count calendar could pinpoint any single day across a span of over 5,000 years using a positional system of kins, uinals, tuns, katuns, and baktuns, unmatched by any other pre-modern civilization.
The Popol Vuh describes three failed attempts at creating humans, first from mud, then from wood, before the gods succeeded with maize dough, connecting every corn harvest to cosmic creation.
Only four Maya codices survive from what was once a vast library tradition of bark-paper books, the rest destroyed by tropical decay and Spanish burning.

title: Contact and the Last Kingdom
bullets: The Spanish conquest of the Yucatan took over 170 years because the Maya had no emperor to capture and no capital to seize, just dozens of independent kingdoms each requiring separate conquest.
Francisco de Montejo launched three campaigns against the Yucatan Maya starting in 1527, failing twice before his son exploited the Xiu-Cocom feud to establish Spanish control in 1542.

Bishop Diego de Landa burned thousands of Maya manuscripts at Mani in 1562, then paradoxically wrote the most detailed 16th-century account of Maya culture, including the partial alphabet key that aided decipherment centuries later.
Of the vast Maya literary tradition, only four codices survive: the Dresden, Madrid, Paris, and Grolier, four books from a civilization that produced books for over a thousand years.

K'iche' Maya nobles transcribed the Popol Vuh into the Latin alphabet around 1554, preserving the most complete account of Maya creation mythology before the original hieroglyphic manuscript was lost.

The Itza Maya on the island city of Nojpeten maintained their independence, calendar, and religion for 175 years after Montejo's first campaign, the longest resistance to European conquest in the Americas.
On March 13, 1697, a Spanish force under Martin de Ursua crossed Lake Peten Itza in a cannon-armed galeota and conquered Nojpeten, the last independent Maya state.

Roughly 6 million Maya people live today in Guatemala, Mexico, Belize, and Honduras, speaking over 30 languages and farming the same milpas of maize, beans, and squash as their ancestors three thousand years ago.
